name: SN2 Reaction
subtitle: Bimolecular Nucleophilic Substitution
conditions: Polar aprotic solvent (DMF, DMSO, acetone, THF); strong nucleophile
nucleophile: Strong, polarisable: I⁻, RS⁻, CN⁻, N₃⁻, RO⁻, Br⁻
leaving_group: I⁻ > OTs > OMs > Br⁻ > Cl⁻ ≫ F⁻, OH⁻
stereochemistry: Complete inversion at the reaction centre (Walden inversion)
key_points: Concerted — single step, no intermediates, one transition state
Backside attack: nucleophile approaches 180° to the leaving group
All three remaining substituents invert (umbrella flip)
Polar aprotic solvents essential — protic solvents H-bond and deactivate the nucleophile
Steric bulk at the α-carbon slows the rate dramatically
notes: The definitive signature of SN2 is second-order kinetics combined with complete stereochemical inversion. Switching from a polar aprotic to a protic solvent can push a borderline substrate entirely into SN1 territory. The reaction is highly sensitive to α-branching: neopentyl systems are essentially inert despite being primary.

name: SN1 Reaction
subtitle: Unimolecular Nucleophilic Substitution
conditions: Polar protic solvent (H₂O, ROH, AcOH); weak or no nucleophile
leaving_group: Same as SN2: I⁻ > OTs > OMs > Br⁻ > Cl⁻
stereochemistry: Racemisation (planar carbocation + two-sided attack), often partial inversion due to ion-pair shielding
kinetics: 1st order: rate = k[RX] — nucleophile not in rate law
key_points: Two-step: ionisation → carbocation (rate-determining), then nucleophilic capture
Carbocation stability governs rate: 3° > 2° > 1°; allylic, benzylic accelerated by resonance
Polar protic solvents stabilise developing charges via H-bonding
Carbocation rearrangements (hydride/methyl shifts) are diagnostic
Ion pair formation can give partial inversion even for SN1
notes: The carbocation intermediate is the key feature distinguishing SN1 from SN2. Its planarity allows nucleophilic attack from both faces, giving racemisation as a hallmark product ratio. However, in practice, the departing leaving group partially shields one face (ion pair), so you often observe predominantly inverted product alongside the racemic background.

name: Finkelstein Reaction
subtitle: Halide Exchange via SN2
conditions: NaI in dry acetone; reflux
substrate_scope: Primary alkyl chlorides and bromides; secondary possible
key_points: Equilibrium driven to product by insolubility of NaCl or NaBr in acetone
NaI is soluble in acetone; NaCl/NaBr precipitate — Le Chatelier drives reaction forward
Converts alkyl chlorides and bromides to more reactive iodides
Useful to activate sluggish chlorides for subsequent reactions
Proceeds by SN2: only works for primary and some secondary halides
notes: The Finkelstein reaction is thermodynamically elegant: the driving force is entirely the differential solubility of the sodium halide salts in acetone. NaI dissolves; NaBr and NaCl precipitate. This shifts the equilibrium cleanly toward the iodide product. The resulting alkyl iodide is a more reactive electrophile for further transformations.

name: E2 Elimination
subtitle: Bimolecular Elimination — Zaitsev and Hofmann
conditions: Strong base (KOH, KOtBu, NaH); heat; protic or aprotic solvent
substrate_scope: 2° and 3° alkyl halides; 1° halides with very strong base
stereochemistry: Anti-periplanar geometry required — H and LG must be 180° coplanar
key_points: Concerted: base abstracts β-H simultaneously as C–LG bond breaks
Anti-periplanar geometry mandatory — sets regiochemistry via conformational analysis
Zaitsev product: more substituted (thermodynamic) alkene — small, strong base (KOH)
Hofmann product: less substituted alkene — bulky base (KOtBu) due to steric approach
Competing with SN2/SN1 — favoured by heat, bulky base, tertiary substrate
notes: The regiochemical outcome (Zaitsev vs Hofmann) is determined by the steric bulk of the base. KOH abstracts from the most accessible, most acidic β-position (leading to the more stable alkene). KOtBu, being sterically congested, is forced to abstract from the least hindered β-hydrogen, giving the Hofmann product. Anti-periplanarity is absolute — the molecule must adopt a conformation where H and LG are 180° apart.

name: E1 Elimination
subtitle: Unimolecular Elimination
conditions: Polar protic solvent; weak base or water; heat favours elimination over substitution
stereochemistry: No geometric requirement — rotation around C–C bond is free after ionisation
kinetics: 1st order: rate = k[RX] — base not in rate law
key_points: Two-step: ionisation → carbocation (rate-limiting), then β-proton loss
Shares the carbocation intermediate with SN1 — competition governed by conditions
Always gives Zaitsev product (most stable alkene, no steric restriction on proton loss)
Heat strongly favours elimination over SN1 (entropy-driven)
Carbocation rearrangements can intervene before elimination
notes: E1 and SN1 always compete because they share the same carbocation intermediate. Temperature is the dominant handle: elevated temperature drives E1 (more molecules, entropy gain from the alkene); room temperature or excess nucleophile favours SN1. Unlike E2, no geometric constraint operates after the carbocation forms, so all accessible β-protons are available.

name: Hydrohalogenation
subtitle: Markovnikov Addition of HX to Alkenes
conditions: HX (gaseous or in inert solvent); no peroxides
substrate_scope: Any alkene; unsymmetrical alkenes give regioselective products
stereochemistry: Racemisation — carbocation intermediate allows attack from both faces
kinetics: Rate depends on alkene nucleophilicity and HX acidity
key_points: Markovnikov's rule: H adds to the less-substituted carbon (more stable carbocation forms)
Electrophile (H⁺) adds first → carbocation intermediate → halide captures
More substituted carbocation = more stable = kinetically preferred
Anti-Markovnikov with HBr/peroxides (radical mechanism, homolytic)
Vinyl and aryl halides not obtained by this route
notes: Markovnikov's rule is fundamentally a statement about carbocation stability: protonation occurs at the carbon that generates the more stable (more substituted) carbocation. In the presence of ROOR (peroxides), HBr adds via a radical chain mechanism with opposite regioselectivity (anti-Markovnikov) — a completely different pathway that does not apply to HCl or HI.

name: Halogenation of Alkenes
subtitle: Anti Addition of X₂ via Cyclic Halonium
conditions: Br₂ or Cl₂ in CH₂Cl₂ or CCl₄; no light, no peroxides
stereochemistry: Strictly anti addition — nucleophile attacks from opposite face to halogen
key_points: Bridged halonium ion intermediate (3-membered ring with positive halogen)
Anti addition is absolute: backside attack on the halonium gives trans product
For cyclic alkenes: addition gives diaxial product (trans-dihalide)
Br₂ reacts faster than Cl₂; I₂ is reversible; F₂ too violent
Bromine water decolorisation is the classic alkene test
notes: The cyclic halonium ion is the mechanistic key. The halogen bridges both carbons from one face, forcing the nucleophilic halide to attack from the opposite face — guaranteeing anti addition. This means a cis-alkene gives the (R,S)/(S,R) enantiomers, while a trans-alkene gives the (R,R)/(S,S) pair (meso compound when substituents are identical). The geometry of the starting alkene is directly readable in the product stereochemistry.

name: Hydroboration–Oxidation
subtitle: Anti-Markovnikov syn Hydration of Alkenes
general_scheme: R–CH=CH₂  →[1. BH₃·THF]→  R–CH₂–CH₂–BH₂  →[2. H₂O₂ / NaOH]→  R–CH₂–CH₂–OH
conditions: 1. BH₃·THF or 9-BBN in THF; 2. H₂O₂, NaOH, H₂O
substrate_scope: Terminal and internal alkenes; 9-BBN for bulky/selective applications
stereochemistry: syn addition — B and H delivered from the same face; net retention at C bearing OH
key_points: Anti-Markovnikov: boron adds to the less hindered (less substituted) carbon
syn addition: both B and H add to the same face of the alkene
Oxidation step (H₂O₂/NaOH) replaces B with OH with retention of configuration
Overall: anti-Markovnikov hydration with syn selectivity
9-BBN gives superior selectivity with bulky substrates
notes: Hydroboration is one of the most versatile reactions in synthesis because it gives the anti-Markovnikov alcohol with defined syn stereochemistry — the complement of acid-catalysed hydration (Markovnikov, racemised) and OsO₄ dihydroxylation (syn diol). The boron acts as an electrophile toward the alkene π-system in a four-centred transition state. The stereochemical outcome (syn, anti-Markovnikov) is locked in at this step.

name: Epoxidation
subtitle: syn Delivery of Oxygen via Peracid
conditions: mCPBA (or mCPBA, DMDO, Oxone) in CH₂Cl₂ at 0 °C to RT; buffer optional
substrate_scope: Any alkene — electron-rich alkenes react faster; sensitive to steric factors
stereochemistry: syn — oxygen added to one face; alkene geometry preserved in epoxide
key_points: Concerted butterfly transition state — O is delivered in a single step
Electron-rich alkenes react faster (peracid is electrophilic oxygen source)
cis-Alkene → cis-epoxide; trans-alkene → trans-epoxide (stereospecific)
Epoxides are opened under acidic (anti, Markovnikov) or basic (anti, SN2) conditions
Sharpless asymmetric epoxidation uses Ti(OiPr)₄/DIPT for allylic alcohols
notes: The peracid delivers oxygen via a concerted electrophilic mechanism — no cationic intermediate forms, which is why the reaction is strictly stereospecific. The facial selectivity is controlled by steric factors (the peracid approaches from the less hindered face). The resulting epoxide is a versatile electrophile: ring opening under acidic conditions follows Markovnikov regioselectivity (attack at more substituted C), while basic conditions follow SN2 (attack at less substituted C).

name: Grignard Addition
subtitle: Nucleophilic Addition to Carbonyl Compounds
conditions: Anhydrous ether (Et₂O or THF); strictly anhydrous conditions; then aqueous workup
substrate_scope: Aldehydes (→ 2° alcohol), ketones (→ 3° alcohol), esters (→ 3° alcohol, 2 eq), CO₂ (→ RCOOH), nitriles (→ ketone)
stereochemistry: Racemisation when a new stereocentre is generated; facial selectivity possible with chiral auxiliaries
key_points: Grignard reagent (RMgX) is a powerful carbon nucleophile — effectively R:⁻
Adds irreversibly to C=O; protonated in aqueous workup
Water, alcohol, acid, CO₂ — all protonate or destroy the Grignard before addition
Esters react twice (2 equiv. Grignard) → tertiary alcohol
Grignard + CO₂ → RCOOMgX → RCOOH after workup (carboxylation)
notes: The Grignard reaction is one of the most powerful tools for C–C bond formation. The key limitation is the absolute intolerance for protic functional groups (–OH, –NH, –COOH, –CHO in the Grignard component) which protonate the reagent. Preparation requires anhydrous, oxygen-free conditions. The order of reactivity toward Grignard reagents: acyl chloride > ester ≈ aldehyde > ketone > CO₂ > nitrile.

name: Aldol Condensation
subtitle: C–C Bond Formation via Enolate or Enol
conditions: Base (NaOH, LDA for kinetic enolate) or acid; aqueous or organic solvent
substrate_scope: Aldehydes and ketones with α-H; crossed aldol with one partner lacking α-H (Claisen–Schmidt)
stereochemistry: New stereocentre at β-carbon; controlled via Zimmermann–Traxler TS with LDA/boron enolates
key_points: Base-catalysed: enolate is the nucleophile; acid-catalysed: enol tautomer attacks
Addition product: β-hydroxy carbonyl compound (aldol product)
Condensation (dehydration at 100 °C or base/heat) gives α,β-unsaturated carbonyl
Crossed aldol: use one carbonyl with no α-H as electrophile (formaldehyde, benzaldehyde)
Mukaiyama aldol: silyl enol ether + Lewis acid gives aldol under mild, controlled conditions
notes: The aldol reaction is foundational in synthesis — it builds carbon skeletons with simultaneous introduction of oxygenated functionality. The condensation step (loss of water) is favoured when the resulting enone is conjugated. In the directed (kinetic) variant, LDA at –78 °C generates the enolate without equilibration; the subsequent cyclic Zimmermann–Traxler transition state controls the relative stereochemistry of the new stereocentres.

name: Fischer Esterification
subtitle: Acid-Catalysed Condensation of Carboxylic Acid and Alcohol
conditions: Cat. H₂SO₄ or p-TsOH; excess alcohol or removal of water; reflux
substrate_scope: Carboxylic acids + primary/secondary alcohols; tertiary alcohols give elimination
mechanism: Nucleophilic acyl substitution: protonation of C=O → tetrahedral intermediate → loss of H₂O → ester
key_points: Reversible equilibrium — driven by excess alcohol or azeotropic water removal (Dean-Stark)
Labelling studies: acyl–oxygen bond is cleaved, not alkyl–oxygen
Tertiary alcohols give alkene (E1) instead of ester under acidic conditions
The reverse reaction (saponification) with NaOH/H₂O is irreversible (forms RCOO⁻)
Acyl chloride + alcohol is faster and irreversible — preferred in synthesis
notes: Fischer esterification is mechanistically the simplest acyl substitution: the acid protonates the carbonyl, activating it toward the weakly nucleophilic alcohol. Because the reaction is reversible, equilibrium must be driven to the right. In industrial settings, water is continuously removed. In the lab, a Dean-Stark trap or molecular sieves are common. For acid-sensitive substrates, DCC coupling or acyl chloride formation are preferred.

name: Wittig Reaction
subtitle: Olefination of Aldehydes and Ketones
conditions: Phosphonium ylide (Ph₃P=CR₂); THF or Et₂O; −78 °C to RT; no water needed
substrate_scope: Aldehydes and ketones; stabilised ylides give E-alkenes; unstabilised give Z
stereochemistry: Unstabilised ylides (Ph₃P=CH₂): predominantly Z; stabilised (Ph₃P=CHCO₂Et): predominantly E
key_points: Ylide is the carbon nucleophile: carbanion stabilised by adjacent P⁺
Mechanism: [2+2] cycloaddition → oxaphosphetane → retro [2+2] → alkene + Ph₃P=O
Converts C=O directly to C=C — unique specificity, no over-reduction
By-product is Ph₃P=O — strong P=O bond drives the reaction irreversibly
Horner–Wadsworth–Emmons variant (phosphonate ester): better E selectivity, water-soluble by-product
notes: The Wittig reaction is one of the most reliable methods for alkene synthesis because the position of the new double bond is completely defined by the carbonyl and ylide used — no regiochemical ambiguity. The stereoselectivity depends on the nature of the ylide: reactive (non-stabilised) ylides react via a kinetically controlled Z-selective pathway; stabilised ylides equilibrate and give the thermodynamic E product. The irreversibility is assured by formation of Ph₃P=O, one of the strongest P=O bonds in organic chemistry.

name: Diels–Alder Reaction
subtitle: [4+2] Thermal Cycloaddition
conditions: Heat (thermal, 80–200 °C) or Lewis acid catalyst (AlCl₃, BF₃·Et₂O); varies with diene/dienophile
substrate_scope: s-cis diene + electron-poor dienophile (EWG on dienophile accelerates); inverse electron demand possible
stereochemistry: Syn addition on both components; endo rule favours kinetic endo product; stereospecific
key_points: Diene must be in s-cis conformation — s-trans diene is unreactive
Electron-withdrawing groups on dienophile accelerate reaction (LUMO lowering)
Endo product kinetically preferred (secondary orbital interactions)
Completely stereospecific: cis dienophile substituents → cis in product
HOMO(diene)–LUMO(dienophile) interaction drives the normal electron-demand reaction
notes: The Diels–Alder reaction constructs two C–C bonds and up to four stereocentres in a single step — remarkable efficiency. The endo rule (kinetic preference for endo transition state due to secondary orbital interactions) gives predictable facial selectivity. In practice, Lewis acid catalysis dramatically lowers the required temperature and can confer high enantioselectivity when chiral ligands are used. Retro-Diels–Alder (retro-[4+2]) is equally important for unmasking dienes at elevated temperatures.

name: Beckmann Rearrangement
subtitle: Oxime → Amide (Lactam) via Nitrene-like Migration
conditions: Strong acid (H₂SO₄, polyphosphoric acid) or PCl₅; 80–120 °C
substrate_scope: Ketoximes (from ketones); regioselective — anti group migrates
mechanism: Oxime protonation → loss of OH⁻ → anti-group migrates to N → nitrilium → hydration → amide
key_points: The group anti to the OH on the oxime migrates (migration is stereospecific)
Migration is concerted with departure of the leaving group — no free nitrene
Cyclohexanone → ε-caprolactam (industrial nylon-6 precursor)
Mixed ketones give regioselective products based on oxime geometry
Oxime geometry can be set by choice of conditions during oxime formation
notes: The Beckmann rearrangement has massive industrial significance — the conversion of cyclohexanone oxime to ε-caprolactam (using oleum) is the key step in nylon-6 production. The mechanistic feature that makes it useful in synthesis is its absolute anti-specificity: the group anti to –OH is the migrating group, so the outcome is predictable from the known oxime geometry. This allows access to amides not easily synthesised by other routes.

name: PCC Oxidation
subtitle: Pyridinium Chlorochromate — Mild Alcohol Oxidation
conditions: PCC (1.5–2 eq) in CH₂Cl₂; RT; anhydrous
substrate_scope: Primary alcohols → aldehydes; secondary alcohols → ketones; tertiary: no reaction
key_points: Stops at aldehyde — water-free conditions prevent over-oxidation to carboxylic acid
Mild: does not affect most other functional groups
Tertiary alcohols not oxidised (no α-H for elimination)
PDC (pyridinium dichromate) is an alternative with similar selectivity
Swern or Dess–Martin periodinane preferred for sensitive substrates
notes: PCC is the workhorse mild oxidant for converting primary alcohols to aldehydes without proceeding to the carboxylic acid — the key is the absence of water, which would protonate the chromate ester and allow over-oxidation. The mechanism proceeds through a chromate ester that undergoes E2-like elimination. For more sensitive substrates (amino alcohols, unsaturated systems), Swern oxidation (DMSO/oxalyl chloride, −78 °C) or Dess–Martin periodinane offer cleaner profiles.

name: Swern Oxidation
subtitle: DMSO-Based Mild Oxidation of Alcohols
conditions: DMSO activated by oxalyl chloride at −78 °C; base (Et₃N) added last; warm to RT
substrate_scope: Primary alcohols → aldehydes; secondary alcohols → ketones; extremely mild, broad compatibility
mechanism: Oxalyl chloride activates DMSO → chlorosulfonium ion; alcohol displaces → alkoxysulfonium → Et₃N abstracts β-H → elimination → carbonyl + Me₂S
key_points: −78 °C for activation — prevents Pummerer rearrangement side products
Et₃N is added last to initiate elimination (must be at low temperature)
Very mild — compatible with acid-sensitive, base-sensitive, and unsaturated substrates
By-products: CO, CO₂ (gas), Me₂S (volatile) — easy removal
Dess–Martin periodinane (DMP) is an alternative without odorous Me₂S
notes: Swern oxidation is the method of choice for oxidising sensitive substrates because it operates at −78 °C and uses no metal oxidants. The critical sequence is: DMSO + oxalyl chloride at −78 °C → add alcohol → add base (Et₃N). Warming before adding base leads to Pummerer rearrangement. The dimethyl sulphide by-product is volatile and foul-smelling — conduct in a well-ventilated fume hood and quench with H₂O₂ if needed.

name: Ozonolysis
subtitle: Oxidative Cleavage of C=C Double Bonds
conditions: O₃ in CH₂Cl₂ or MeOH at −78 °C; workup determines products
substrate_scope: Any alkene; internal give two fragments; terminal give aldehyde + formaldehyde
key_points: Reductive workup (Me₂S, PPh₃, Zn/AcOH): aldehydes and ketones
Oxidative workup (H₂O₂): aldehydes further oxidised → carboxylic acids; ketones unchanged
Mechanism: [3+2] cycloaddition (molozonide) → rearrangement → carbonyl oxide + carbonyl → recombine → ozonide
Ozonide is explosive — warm carefully; reductive workup minimises risk
Useful for structure determination (fragmentation pattern reveals alkene position)
notes: Ozonolysis is the most reliable method for cleaving a C=C bond. The choice of workup is mechanistically simple but practically critical: Me₂S or PPh₃ reduces the carbonyl oxide to give the carbonyl compound; H₂O₂ oxidises it — so primary carbons give carboxylic acids under oxidative conditions. The ozonide intermediate is a peroxide and potentially explosive upon concentration; always work at low temperature and destroy excess ozone with reductant before warming.

name: NaBH₄ Reduction
subtitle: Selective Hydride Reduction of Aldehydes and Ketones
conditions: NaBH₄ in MeOH, EtOH, or THF/H₂O; 0 °C to RT; mild aqueous workup
substrate_scope: Aldehydes > ketones — does not reduce esters, amides, carboxylic acids, alkenes under normal conditions
stereochemistry: Facial selectivity from steric approach — equatorial attack favoured in cyclohexanones (Baeyer–Villiger product profile)
key_points: Delivers hydride (H⁻) from boron to electrophilic carbonyl carbon
Aldehydes more reactive than ketones — selectivity possible with careful stoichiometry
Does NOT reduce esters, amides, or carboxylic acids under standard conditions
Reduces iminium ions and N-acyliminium ions efficiently
L-Selectride (LiBH(sec-Bu)₃) gives superior steric facial selectivity
notes: NaBH₄ is the reagent of choice for straightforward ketone and aldehyde reduction due to its ease of handling (stable in protic solvents) and functional group tolerance. Its selectivity over esters and amides allows chemoselective reduction of complex molecules. For challenging facial selectivity, the choice of bulky hydride (L-Selectride for equatorial attack, K-Selectride for axial) and solvent (coordinating vs non-coordinating) are the key variables.

name: LiAlH₄ Reduction
subtitle: Powerful Hydride Reduction — Full Functional Group Arsenal
conditions: LiAlH₄ in anhydrous Et₂O or THF; 0 °C to reflux; careful aqueous workup (Fieser: H₂O, 15%NaOH, H₂O in ratio)
substrate_scope: Carboxylic acids → 1° alcohol; esters → 2× alcohol; aldehydes/ketones → alcohol; amides → amine; nitriles → amine
key_points: Much more powerful than NaBH₄ — reduces esters, amides, carboxylic acids
Absolutely anhydrous conditions required — violent reaction with H₂O
Workup: Fieser quench (add mL water = mass of LAH used, then 15% NaOH, then more water)
Reduces carboxylic acids (NaBH₄ cannot) — key advantage
DIBAL-H at −78 °C reduces esters to aldehydes (stops at aldehyde stage)
notes: LiAlH₄ is the most powerful common hydride reductant. Its high reactivity means it reduces nearly every electrophilic carbonyl, making it less selective than NaBH₄ but essential for reductions that NaBH₄ cannot achieve. The workup must be done carefully — water addition is exothermic and releases H₂ gas; Fieser's method (mL H₂O = mass LAH in grams) is reliable. For chemoselective reductions of complex molecules with multiple reducible groups, alternative hydrides (DIBAL, Red-Al, 9-BBN reductant) should be considered.

name: Electrophilic Aromatic Substitution — Nitration
subtitle: Arenium Ion Mechanism; Regioselectivity via Directing Effects
conditions: Conc. HNO₃ + conc. H₂SO₄ (mixed acid); 0–60 °C depending on substrate
mechanism: H₂SO₄ protonates HNO₃ → generates NO₂⁺ (nitronium); electrophilic attack → arenium (Wheland) intermediate → proton loss restores aromaticity
key_points: NO₂⁺ (nitronium ion) is the active electrophile, generated from HNO₃/H₂SO₄
Ortho/para directors: +I or +M groups (alkyl, OH, OR, NHR) — activate the ring
Meta directors: −M groups (NO₂, CN, COR, SO₃H, NR₃⁺) — deactivate the ring
Halogen substituents: ortho/para directors but deactivators (−I, +M balance)
Rate-determining step: electrophilic attack (formation of arenium), not proton loss
notes: Electrophilic aromatic substitution encompasses nitration, halogenation, sulfonation, Friedel–Crafts acylation and alkylation. The regioselectivity rule is straightforward: electron-donating groups (via resonance or induction) stabilise ortho/para arenium intermediates → ortho/para product; electron-withdrawing groups destabilise ortho/para → meta product. Steric effects modify the ortho/para ratio (bulky groups favour para). Halobenzenes are a classic exception: halogens withdraw electrons inductively (−I, making the ring less reactive) but donate via resonance (+M, directing ortho/para).
